speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four, Lilly Frank Grossman. Sorry. OK. So you've arrived in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived in Stutthof. And that was very good after Auschwitz, to sleep in a bed, on straw bed. But it was bed, you could call it. And it was water. We could drink water. We could wash our face.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a lager was Stutthof, compared to Auschwitz? How did it look?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it is very different.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we have some hygienic things, like washing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have washrooms? Or was still latrines?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- no, we have a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Running water?

speaker: interviewee
text: --washroom, running water.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had running water there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We have a big room where we could use. Not shower, but it was something there. And the water came, so we could wash ourself all over. And we have-- we found lots of things. So we had towel. So that was a big things. And we have to stay this place 10 in a line. So our five, and other five came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there also Block Altestes there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, it-- oh, it--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the same-- the same set up, the same routine?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know it was a [NON-ENGLISH] there.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] ?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple. Terrible. You know, we called [NON-ENGLISH], who hang up people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hangman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hangman. It was like him. We called him [NON-ENGLISH], hangman. You know what he did? He have a box gloves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boxing gloves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boxing gloves. And I don't know how he did, but it was 10 people facing him in one line. Facing. He gave a slap for the first one, and all the three went down. I don't know how he did, but he did. And it's true. All 10 of us was falling backwards. Otherwise, we got soup in our own bowl. But the soup was from grass, and not vegetables, some weeds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sticks and--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was it. Sometimes, it was a little corn in. It was Auschwitz, worse. And we got lots of brome to keep us quiet. And we lost our period, all of us, everybody, from this brome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know it was from the brome? How-- when-- how did you eat the brome? What was it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the soup, in the food, in the soup, or in the coffee also, I don't know. But we know it was a very strange smell and taste also.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in Stutthof a few weeks. That was send anywhere, because the-- the sea was close. That was Danzig, otherwise. But they called Stutthof that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they have-- we did-- we didn't know, just later, they had a electric [? bot, ?] not gas, electric [? bot. ?] The people who was taken over there, it was on the floor. And they shoot-- hold something, and they were electrosed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Electrocuted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They told us later, the Ukraine guards, whom they-- from Botten, later us, took up over there people. And one of them, my cousin, wanted to go. And the older guy was very nice. The name was [? Chernyl, ?] which means black in Russian. He was a very good guy. He liked my mother. He liked my mother. But he has a younger brother, 17 or 20 years old. We called him 'Little Murderer,' because for one pack of cigarette daily, he killed 20 women-- for a pack of cigarette. We called him 'Little Murderer.' And the older one--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he kill them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shot them, one by one, quickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 Minutes. Heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Heard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was this time in the [NON-ENGLISH], the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a bread--

speaker: interviewee
text: --bread--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ovens, and the bakery.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, bakery. Oven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. The ration-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This is-- was five or six of us, and we gave out the ration daily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The margarine, the jam, or whatever. That was next. We call [INAUDIBLE]. It was like two, three big living room together from wood, but we have a little stove there. And we could make warm. I was chosen, because they saw me to work with the potato. And one of the-- OK, I am jump now. OK. I go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for Stutthof, end of August, in my estimate, between 3,000, 5,000 women arrived in a small village on a farm, [? Botten. ?] [? Botten ?] is between Torun and [? Culm. ?] One more city was there. I can't remember. Maybe I could find out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a big farm. It was a beautiful house for the owner, a baron. And it was another building for kitchen and washing places for the employees and for the warehouse for us, and the German, and the guards. And they were living there. And we were taken in a wooden building, which is very, very big, very cold. It was from lumber. And because that has to be airy, so it was holes on it to air moving, because they kept over there the hay, the straw, many things for the farm animals. And they put straw down on the soil, simple soil, no wood, nothing, no wood, nothing. And we were just like animal to shovel in this place. So again, like sardines, we five get together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next day, they let us to go out for walk. We didn't know are we doing, OK? We just went, because they told us to go. So we went out, a few-- excuse me-- a few kilometer. It was still dark. It was cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was only early September, but the rain season started. We were rained out. We were going in mud, heavily. The Ukraine guards, whom the Germans are collected and bring in Poland, they have a belt. And on their belt, they said [NON-ENGLISH], God is with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: God is with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were right, believe me, because the God was only with us, not with us. Is no question.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went out to work. And we started to dig [NON-ENGLISH] traps. Traps--

speaker: interviewee
text: How could I explain to you? It was about six, seven--

speaker: interviewer
text: Trenches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trenches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Trenches.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but it was about 12, 15 meter wide. It was going down that way, and the bottom was only four meter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's narrower at the bottom.

speaker: interviewee
text: But just wandering about with that food, to get out the soil from that place. The Wehrmacht was with us. The Ukraine came with us five, six, seven kilometer, but they went to sleep. And the Wehrmacht was taking care of us. They were good. German, but Wehrmacht, not SS. The SS was the officers. And that was working out for a while. But because of-- we didn't see water, only when it was raining. We didn't wash up, never. We didn't have food, good. Every day to come and go 10, 12 kilometers walking in the mud, to stand out at-- at early morning and at night, until they counted us. And then the people started to die on the road, out and in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they brought in the dead people. They counted them. They counted us. And it was OK. Then they let us to go to the kitchen for a bowl of soup. But the Ukraine guard, famous, pleasure was to get a long stick. And they just hit us, so the bowl and the soup was flying. So then, we went to the big barn, or whatever. But it was huge. I can't explain how huge it was and how cold it was. So it was already lice-- lice and lice everywhere, even in our eyebrow. Our hair started to grow in, our underarm, everywhere, lice in the sweater, lice on our-- our body, everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got very little lice. I don't know why. Everybody has, and I still doesn't have. Finally, when I get, one day I figured my nipple get infected. The lice was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Chewing on them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chewing on. And [NON-ENGLISH], it get inflammation, you know? And under my underarm was like a eggs, a gland. So that was almost the correct [? death row. ?] But at morning, the oberscharfuhrer-- Meier, was the name-- was standing there with his officers and two line of soldiers, the Ukraines, with piece of wood, with the gun, with the shovels, whatever your could name.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the person who wanted to stay home because was sick should walk up and tell the Meier, the oberscharfuhrer, I am sick. I can't work. I have fever, or I have the frozen feet, or whatever. If Meier said stay, they stayed. If not, the person has to walk back. And when she got the end of the line, the Ukraines, he was dead, beaten up, pieces of bloody places. So what happened with you, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said goodbye to my mother, because I really was in trouble. I couldn't lift up my hand. And all there, the Wehrmacht-- they were in Silesia-- asked me why did I came in a smoking jacket. It was cold already.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know what he did? He saved some soup. They got lunchtime, the soup. We were sitting down at a half an hour, eat our bread or whatever. And he brought the soup for my mother. So I [? shared, ?] I [? shared ?] I got. And my five people, we are get two spoon everybody. Everybody shared.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what happened with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said goodbye for everybody, and I went out. And I was a very sporty person. So I didn't lost my weight so much, because I was all over muscle. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very fit.

speaker: interviewee
text: My strength was-- I was a fit, of steel, fitted. It was another Krakovic, Lili, with our five. She was a very, very fat girl. And she lost weight, like that. So over there, I was the fat Lili, and she was the skinny Lili. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went out, and I button up my dress. And I show my breast and my underarm to that guy. And between there, so many [NON-ENGLISH] collection-- OK, skeletons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Skeleton, I still was looking almost--

speaker: interviewer
text: Normal.

speaker: interviewee
text: --normal, and a young girl. So he was watching me like that. And told me, stay. But could you believe what was this moment to my mother there, and for me walking up?

speaker: interviewer
text: Very brave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, ever, anybody. Wherever is hate, everybody should fight against it, because the hate is the most terrible things on the whole world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get treatment for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the funniest, funniest, [NON-ENGLISH], funniest part. So it was potato, like a hill on the middle of the yard. And we have wire basket. And we have fork and-- big one, you know, like in the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pitchfork.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pitchfork? OK, pitchfork. It was a button, a little things at the end. So when we collect the potato, we didn't go in, because that little things all were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the girls went up on the potato and started from the top to collect some potato in the basket to put in the barn to cleaning. Some other people was cleaning potato for the kitchen, for the night, for dinner, you know? So I was a village girl. We had farm. And I was pleading them, please don't go up. Start at the bottom-- just the fork, in, fork, in the basket.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other two girls, they took it-- I'm sorry. Too much memories. [DRINKS ] They didn't listen to me, until the guard came and beat them up. They doesn't want to another Jewish girl get any instruction. I know how to do it. And my heart went out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one of the Silesia, I think, [NON-ENGLISH], Herr Bader-- we just called him yellow officer, because he has a yellow uniform. And Friedrich, who was with the potato cleaning, was the grey officer-- we didn't know their name-- came over there, and he saw me to working. And you know the German. Work is the most important.

speaker: interviewee
text: He saw me to work and came over there. I didn't know German-- a little from school. Maybe somebody translated, or not. He asked me why I am home if I could work like that. So I said, I have trouble. OK, show me. So again, open up. Lift up. So he himself took me in the ambulance, or emergency, or hospital, whatever, another big [NON-ENGLISH], like the [NON-ENGLISH] exactly. There were people on straw, lying down, half dead. And I think a barber came every week, once. He once, I saw-- I saw-- cut over the ankle somebody's leg, because it was frozen. No anesthesia, no frozen, nothing, just a sharp knife, he cut. And he put on some gaze.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gauze.

speaker: interviewee
text: From paper. Paper. So what that yellow officer did, he took me before everybody. And two, three girls was over there, who took care of the people. So he asked them to put some cream on me and put me heavy, heavy paper, gaze, you know? But it wasn't gaze, it was from paper. So I was packed up, bandaged up, with a whole day's gaze, which could be go for everybody. Not so long time after that, two hours later, the gray guy came. But I think they wanted to see a woman, a normal body of a woman. In the meantime, I run in the ambulance. They took off the gaze from me, just left a little, little. Because I knew he's coming. And he took me to the ambulance, put cream on me, and put again all the gaze on me. And then, he was happy. They didn't want anything from me. Just to see me, they gave me a correct treatment. People hate me. People hate me for that-- but I was absolutely innocent-- Because I have a special care. I have a special care.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went back again. And they took off me the gaze-- the gaze and then treated the other sick people. It was a torture for me. I was hated for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working. And all of the sudden, that yellow officer, who took care of the [NON-ENGLISH]-- I always say [NON-ENGLISH], that bread ration cell, we called cell-- took me in the [NON-ENGLISH]. But not Lloyd, the insurance company, has insurance better than it was there. Otherwise, that was the most terrible things what ever happened to me, because my mother still was walking out to work. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This took place in where? In Stutthof? This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that is [? Botten, ?] everything, already.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is already [? Botten? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know if we have time on this tape for her to read this poem. Do you want to read this poem? This is the poem that you wanted to tell us about.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to sing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're-- OK, would you sing it for us now?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain what this is for.

speaker: interviewee
text: That song which comes was made up by the labor camps people. And we got it, somehow, on the grapevine. When the people was crying and close to the nervous breakdown, was a girl, gypsy girl, we called. She was from East Hungary, so she took-- she could talk with the Ukraines, the guards. They were good friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she stood up the middle of the night. I wasn't there. I was sleeping in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And watching out every night when the people came out-- came in from the work, is my mother returned or not? That was the most terrible time. So she stood up. And the guard said, gypsy, start to sing and calm down-- down the people, like a bedtime story. That was-- don't listen to my voice. I don't have good voice. You see, my English is not so good.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I want to-- this song, we don't know who put up this song. And I would like to sing. It is all to go home and see parents and everybody. I want to-- this one to stay, even in Hungary. And somebody is going to translate. It's very emotional.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you do that for us then?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SINGING ] Don't cry, because I could cry also. That is always was in calming us down. Is no question about how emotional was, how it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from [? Botten ?] in January, we went to Grodno. But more than half of the people was dead. The village made the riot, because the dead bodies was piled up, piled up, hundreds of them, frozen. Finally, they made some ditch, and they put over them, because the village was complaining. So they took us from Grodno. From Grodno-- we were staying there two or three weeks-- and we started to walk in January, the 20, in the cold, frozen snow. No food. Nothing. One blanket around us, that's it. Whoever had shoes, had. Who wasn't, that was tough luck. So we started to walking [NON-ENGLISH] 18, 20, 25 kilometers. And every night, we were shoved in a barn. But in the meantime, all on the way, every day, every hour. Some people sit down on the side of the road, and one shotgun, and we said, goodbye Jewish sister. We didn't know who, just one of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that went on, and on, and on. But that was less than half we went over there. I don't think it was 1,000 people, all together. No food, again.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in a, let's say, a beer factory, OK? And they have the potato peel, hot. And that was the luxury dinner for us. They gave you hot potato peels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peel. They didn't have anything else. Whatever they could require, they gave us. Because otherwise, we-- and we ate the snow. Even my mother told me, don't gave me soft. I want to bite something, so frozen snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were going and going. Finally, the very first of March, we-- or end of February, we-- we came to a Hitlerjugend camp. OK. We'll stop now.
